title: RBA in a bind: Fight inflation or risk recession
full_text:
The Reserve Bank of Australia is in an uncomfortable spot and this week’s data will make that even clearer.
Four months after the central bank began an aggressive rate hiking cycle to contain the latest inflation outbreak, the economic data is expected to tell a similar story – prices are growing too fast, but the economy is slowing and jobs are at risk.
The RBA’s monetary policy board says policy is “well placed to respond” to the latest developments.
Yet rate cuts are surely off the table while inflation is out of control, further hikes threaten to tip the economy into a recession and doing nothing risks a damaging bout of stagflation.
Torn between priorities and needing to make uncomfortable choices, the RBA’s monetary policy board finds itself in a “tricky spot”, according to HSBC chief economist Paul Bloxham.
“There is no ‘narrow pathway’ this time,” he says.
After its meeting last week, the board kept the cash rate at a 15-year high of 4.35 per cent as expected after three consecutive rate rises.
However, the board warned that inflation “is still too high” and “demand needs to slow”.
It pointedly left the door open to further rises, saying it would act again “if required”.
The RBA’s most recent publicly available forecasts predict that core inflation won’t hit the midpoint of its 2-3 per cent target band until mid-2028.
If so, core inflation will have been above the target for more than seven years. That’s hardly a satisfactory outcome and one that would risk a significant rise in long-term inflation expectations and an associated wage-price spiral.
With the RBA’s inflation forecast assuming that the cash rate would rise by an additional 35 basis points to 4.7 per cent by December, the only thing that could plausibly lessen the need for further interest rate hikes would be a bigger than expected economic slowdown. Yet after a modest slowing in inflation and employment data last month, the market now sees only a 60 per cent chance of the RBA lifting the cash rate by an additional 25 basis points by November.
For the May inflation data on Wednesday, the main focus will be the trimmed mean CPI – the RBA’s preferred measure of underlying inflation.
HSBC expects it to edge higher year-on-year to 3.5 per cent. One key question is whether higher fuel costs are feeding through to other prices.
Obviously the RBA has no control over energy costs driven by the Middle East conflict. But broad pass-through of those costs would signal that demand remains strong enough to warrant further action.
JPMorgan chief economist Ben Jarman expects headline inflation to hold at 4.2 per cent annually, with monthly prices falling 0.5 per cent.
In his view this would represent a significant undershoot of the RBA’s second-quarter forecast, reflecting both the government’s fuel excise cut and lower global oil prices.
But fuel prices are likely to spike from early July as the excise cut partially expires. That will push headline inflation higher in the second half of the year – though the peak should be lower than initially feared.
May labour force data on Thursday is expected to show a partial rebound after a surprisingly sharp jump to 4.5 per cent in April. Job weakness in April was concentrated among women, with female employment falling sharply – an unusual move that is often reversed the following month.
JPMorgan expects unemployment to dip to 4.4 per cent, with employment rising by 30,000.
But the broader trend is expected to be one of loosening as business confidence is weak, employment intentions have softened and the unemployment rate is likely to keep rising. Economists will be watching youth unemployment and underemployment closely – measures that tend to move more sharply through the cycle than the headline rate.
As RBA governor Michele Bullock noted last week, assessing how tight the labour market is involves more than just the unemployment rate.
The big question is whether the labour market is weakening fast enough to move the dial on inflation.
The RBA has forecast a relatively low unemployment rate of 4.3 per cent by year end.
With the so-called non-inflation accelerating rate of unemployment thought to be about 4.5 per cent, the jobless rate presumably would need to be heading well above that point to crush inflation.
All of this puts the RBA in a bind.
As the central bank has said, the economy needs to slow to bring inflation back to target. That is precisely what the bank has been engineering. Just how hard the landing will be and whether it’s enough to lessen the need for further interest rate hikes is the million-dollar question.
